result:
Thank you for the invitation! I’d like to write a little reflection on the power of curiosity.

Curiosity is such a deceptively simple force. It’s something we associate with children, with wonder, with asking endless “why” questions, or poking things with a stick to see what happens. But, at its heart, curiosity is really the engine of both individuality and collective progress. Every new discovery starts with curiosity: a person glimpses something out of the ordinary, or perhaps dreams up a “what if…?” scenario. The world’s most powerful ideas—from penicillin to poetry, from space travel to sourdough—can draw their roots all the way back to a question. Why does this mold kill bacteria? Why do I feel this emotion? What’s beyond that next hill? Can I make bread rise differently?

Curiosity also wards off stagnation. It’s a renewable resource within us, pushing us to explore, to learn, and to see the world freshly. Perhaps the greatest gift we can give ourselves is to continue nurturing this spark, even in adulthood, when routine and comfort can dull wonder’s edge. Reading, traveling, listening to people from different backgrounds, tasting unfamiliar foods, experimenting with a new hobby—all these are acts of curiosity, both humbling and enlivening. 

There is, of course, productive friction in curiosity, too. Sometimes it leads us to uncomfortable places or upends what we thought we knew. But that’s the rub: a curiosity-driven life is one where change is not an enemy, but a companion, leading us forward.

So if you’re reading this, I hope you’ll let yourself ask more questions, even the small ones—especially the small ones! The answers might surprise you, and the pursuit, in itself, is well worth the journey.
